paper_map.title: Low-Density Steels: Complex Metallurgy for Automotive Applications
paper_map.abstract_summary: This paper reviews the development of low-density steels with high aluminum additions for automotive lightweighting. It examines the effects of Al on phase stability, thermodynamic modeling via CALPHAD, and microstructure evolution, and relates these factors to mechanical properties. The potential of austenite-ferrite and fully austenitic low-density steels is assessed through finite-element crash simulations, demonstrating weight savings of about 20% and 30%, respectively, compared to a DP600 reference.
paper_map.research_objective: To elucidate the complex metallurgical phenomena induced by high aluminum additions in steels and evaluate the weight-saving potential of two low-density steel families for automotive applications through thermodynamic modeling, microstructural characterization, and mechanical performance assessment.
main_process_variables: Finite-element crash simulation conditions (EuroNCAP, piecewise linear plasticity)
key_sections: Introduction and historical use of Al in steel
Effect of Al on steel density
Incorporating Al in Fe-C thermodynamic calculations (CALPHAD)
Microstructural aspects of high-Al steels (recrystallization, cooling microstructures, austenitic grades)
Relation between microstructure and properties (influence of annealing on two-phase microstructures, effect on austenitic properties)
Potential of low-density steels for automotive applications (crash simulations, weight savings)
